IXL Product Proposal: Moving from Supplement to Core Workflow

I interviewed and had multiple working conversations with my mother, a K-6 teacher for 20+ years, now an interventionist for 5 years. The fixes below come from real teacher pain points, then expand to the student, parent, and district needs that decide whether a feature actually gets used.

I created this ixl_report manually first. After I had the core page written, I worked with AI through numerous prompts to create a more fully elaborated companion version that helped flesh out the original report, sharpen the product thinking, and support the demo; that AI-enhanced version is attached as ixl_AI_enhanced.pdf. This page is the original, more organic human-authored report.

Thesis: IXL currently functions as an adaptive supplement. The strategic imperative is to evolve it into the core instructional and homework backbone that teachers actually plan their week around.

That is a direction, not a claim about where the product stands today. The gap this document addresses is the space between IXL as a strong practice tool and IXL as a workflow teachers would actually organize homework and follow-through around.

Classroom excerpt: The clearest through-line from those conversations was not "I need more content," but "I need the platform to save me time while I'm already juggling differentiation, grading, parent communication, and compliance."

Product Direction

Question 2 propositions:
  • SmartScore gap-filling: go to Question 2, item 1 for the auto-differentiated assignment generator that drafts homework from current mastery gaps.
  • Accommodation profiles: go to Question 2, item 2 for how IEP/504 supports are applied automatically at delivery.
  • Struggle signals: go to Question 2, item 3 for time-on-problem alerts and analytics-layer boundaries.
  • Parent digest: go to Question 2, item 4 for plain-language home support and permissioning.
  • District-ready access: go to Question 2, item 5 for Clever, Chromebook visibility, roster sync, and launch dependencies.
Question 3 build decisions:
  • Goals: Question 3a defines the teacher and student outcomes: automated first drafts, reviewable personalization, consolidated progress, persistence, and accommodations.
  • Access: Question 3b defines where Homework appears for teachers and students, plus the difference between classwide add-to-assignment and SmartScore personalization.
  • Data model: Question 3c defines publish-time materialization, roster changes, SmartScore snapshots, accommodation snapshots, and reporting stability.
  • Builder modes: Question 3d defines Manual Build Mode, Auto-Assign Mode, Review State, and Publish Behavior.
  • Distribution: Question 3e defines launch integrations: Clever SSO/roster sync and Chromebook/PWA visibility before broader Google Classroom distribution.

Question 1: Describing IXL to a Teacher

IXL helps solve the parts of practice that usually drain teacher time: finding the right work for different levels, grading it, figuring out who is stuck, and making sure quiet gaps do not slip by unnoticed. It adjusts practice to each student, gives teachers clear progress and mastery signals, and makes differentiation feel manageable without needing a tech-savvy teacher to constantly monitor it. With IXL, teachers spend less time building and checking generic assignments, ultimately freeing up time for higher-impact tasks.

Teacher Time Pressure
A homework workflow has to earn its place inside an already overloaded teacher schedule.
Source: Merrimack College / EdWeek Research Center survey of teachers reporting a typical 54-hour workweek, with 25 hours teaching, 5 hours grading, and 5 hours planning (Education Week, 2022; Merrimack College Teacher Survey, 2023).
References: EdWeek, EdWeek breakdown.
Why Distribution Matters
If homework is going to become a real workflow, it has to live inside existing roster and access systems.
Source: Clever reports that 77% of U.S. K-12 schools use Clever, which makes roster and access compatibility central to district viability, not just convenience (Clever Schools).
Reference: Clever Schools.

Stakeholder Weighting

Each recommendation below is weighted not just by teacher pain, but by who determines whether the feature is adopted, used consistently, and renewed over time.

Teachers
40% Primary workflow owners. If the feature saves time for teachers, it has a chance to become habitual.
Students
25% Student clarity and completion drive the data quality and learning signal the product produces.
Districts
20% District IT, procurement, and compliance needs determine whether the feature can realistically scale.
Parents
15% Parents matter most when homework spills into home support and when product value must be legible outside the classroom.

Question 2: Challenges and Proposed Enhancements

The proposed changes are grounded in teacher archetypes that run into the same problems again and again: a K-5 teacher managing a wide readiness spread, an interventionist building targeted small-group practice, and a secondary teacher who needs progress data that points to the next move. The time-pressure framing comes from teacher-hour data from Education Week and Merrimack.

1. Auto-Differentiated Assignment Generation

Problem: For a classroom teacher or interventionist, the hard part is not knowing that students need different practice. It is finding the time to build below-level, on-level, and advanced versions of the same homework. When that takes too long, the practical fallback is one generalized assignment and a later check to see whether SmartScore moved.

Solution: Add a one-click generator on the teacher dashboard that uses current SmartScore to group students and draft a differentiated homework path for each track. The teacher still reviews, edits, and publishes, but the first draft is no longer manual; that creates faster differentiated homework and a clearer bridge from IXL's mastery data to the work students actually receive.

Build notes: Show the SmartScore ranges behind each group, keep recommendations editable, and make the final publish action teacher-controlled.

Risks / Trade-offs: The main risk is trust. If the reasoning is hidden, teachers may reject the automation; the product should feel like a transparent draft, not an autopilot.

2. Global IEP and 504 Accommodation Profiles

Problem: For teachers serving students with accommodations, the risk is that supports have to be remembered every single time work is assigned. That is fragile. Compliance starts to break down when the workflow depends on repeated human memory.

Solution: Let teachers or authorized staff configure supports once at the student profile level, including extended time, reduced problem count, and audio support. When homework is delivered, those settings apply automatically, which means less manual tracking for teachers and a more consistent experience for students who need support.

Build notes: Permissions, audit history, and district admin controls matter here more than UI polish.

Risks / Trade-offs: This introduces real FERPA and legal sensitivity. If accommodations are applied incorrectly, IXL inherits trust and compliance risk. If it works reliably, though, the product becomes much more credible as school infrastructure.

3. Per-Problem Time Logging and Struggle Alerts

Problem: Accuracy alone does not tell the full story. A student can get answers wrong because they are working hard through a tough skill, or because they are clicking quickly without much engagement.

Solution: Log time-to-answer at the problem level and surface simple alerts when time and accuracy patterns suggest genuine struggle or likely disengagement. This gives teachers better intervention signals than completion alone, plus a clearer way to see who needs help, encouragement, or a different assignment path.

Build notes: Keep raw event data in the analytics layer and show only practical summaries in the teacher workflow.

Risks / Trade-offs: The trade-off is data volume and interpretation. Time data is useful, but it should not overstate what the product can know about a student's intent.

4. Plain-Language Parent Digest

Problem: Parent-facing performance data is often too abstract. A SmartScore can be useful inside IXL, but it does not tell a caregiver what to say or practice that night.

Solution: Send a weekly digest that translates homework progress into plain language: what the student practiced, what improved, and one simple offline prompt or reinforcement action. Parents can support homework without needing to understand the full IXL reporting system.

Build notes: Use plain language, avoid judgmental labels, and respect district communication settings.

Risks / Trade-offs: The risk is communication overreach. Parent messages should be permissioned carefully and default to opt-in in district contexts.

5. Chromebook PWA and Ecosystem Integration

Problem: The most common homework failure mode is often not refusal; it is friction. If assigned work only lives inside IXL and has no visibility in the systems students already use, it is easier to forget and harder to make routine.

Solution: Launch the homework experience with strong distribution: Clever-compatible roster and sign-in support from day one, plus a student-facing entry point that can extend to Chromebook or broader portal visibility. That means fewer access issues before students start and a homework surface that feels connected to the school day instead of hidden inside one product tab.

Build notes: Start with roster and SSO reliability, then add device-level visibility once the core assignment object is stable.

Risks / Trade-offs: Integration work creates dependency on district authentication, review processes, and careful notification design. Still, treating distribution as a later add-on would weaken adoption from the start.

Why these five: They all reduce real teacher workload instead of adding another thing to manage. That is the right filter for an audience already operating under a 50-plus hour workweek.

Question 3: Homework Assignments Spec

Observed current workflow: Today, teachers can send students toward IXL work through individual skills and content discovery. What is missing is a homework engine that uses the information IXL already has, especially SmartScore and progress history, to recommend the optimal mix of skills, lessons, and videos for each student. That leaves IXL strong as a practice engine, but weaker as a full homework workflow that can create personalized assignments automatically and still give teachers a clean review step.

3a. Goals for Teachers and Students

For Teachers

  • Automated first draft: Use SmartScore and existing IXL content to recommend the right skills, lessons, and videos for each student.
  • Time-saving differentiation: Review and adjust personalized sequences without rebuilding homework from scratch.
  • Consolidated reporting: See one assignment-level progress view instead of isolated content completion signals.

For Students

  • Centralized homework destination: Replace scattered suggested work with a clear, unambiguous homework surface.
  • State persistence: Progress saves continuously so interruptions do not reset work.
  • Invisible accommodations: Supports are applied automatically without requiring additional student action.

3b. Access and Navigation

Teacher entry: Put Homework in "At a glance" under "Teaching tools" for fast action, and also give it a permanent home in the Learning dropdown so it feels like a core workflow.

Student entry: Homework should appear as a high-visibility to-do on the student landing page, with teacher, due date, and progress in one place. Students should not have to guess which suggested task is required and which one is optional practice.

Personalization rule: Auto-Assign is the primary path: IXL uses SmartScore to draft student-specific homework from existing skills, lessons, and videos. A quick "Add to Assignment" path should still exist for browsing, but classwide adds must be labeled as non-personalized so the trade-off is clear.

Surface Recommendation Justification
Teacher dashboard Place Homework in "At a glance" under "Teaching tools" This is the easiest access point for a time-constrained teacher and makes the feature feel central instead of buried.
Navbar / Learning Give Homework its own column in the Learning dropdown This gives the feature an official home in the product instead of making it feel like a dashboard-only shortcut.
Teacher browsing flow Allow a quick "Add to Assignment" path while browsing content This supports the way teachers actually move through skills, lessons, and videos, while still preserving the distinction between fast classwide assignment and SmartScore-based personalization.
Student landing page Show a high-visibility homework banner or button This is the clearest way to separate actual homework from suggested or optional practice.
Student device layer Extend Homework into Chromebook PWA visibility or badge-based entry If the assignment state is visible outside the browser tab, the feature has a better chance of becoming part of student routine instead of something they forget to reopen.
Why distribution is part of the product, not an add-on: A homework tool that does not fit district sign-on, roster, and student-device realities can look good in a mockup and still fail in a real school. That is especially true in mid-size and large districts where IT approval shapes what teachers can actually use. Clever, Chromebook, and visibility work should be part of the original concept, not something bolted on later.

3c. Data Model and Architecture

To support automated personalization, mixed-content delivery, and reliable reporting, Homework needs a relational schema that treats assignments as first-class objects with per-student generated paths. It should not just be a loose collection of content links.

Key Decisions:
  • Auto-Assign drafts, teacher publishes. SmartScore and progress history recommend the skills, lessons, and videos; the teacher sees the reasoning and confirms before anything is assigned.
  • Publish creates stable delivery records. Student assignment rows are created at publish time, so homework does not recalculate live from the roster every time a student opens it.
  • Roster changes are explicit. Newly rostered students can be backfilled into active assignments; removed students keep historical records but stop receiving active homework.
  • Results freeze after submission. Final SmartScore, completion, and submitted status are snapshotted so reports stay stable after the due date.
  • Accommodations travel with delivery. IEP/504 settings are applied from the student profile and snapshotted when homework is delivered, so mid-assignment profile changes do not rewrite work already in progress.
  • Analytics stay separate. Raw time-on-problem events live in the analytics layer; teachers see clear struggle summaries, not noisy event logs.
TABLE: assignments
-----------------------------------------
assignment_id      (UUID, Primary Key)
teacher_id         (UUID, Foreign Key)
title              (VARCHAR)
due_date           (TIMESTAMPTZ)
status             (ENUM: draft, active, archived)

TABLE: assignment_items
-----------------------------------------
item_id            (UUID, Primary Key)
assignment_id      (UUID, Foreign Key)
content_type       (ENUM: skill, lesson, video)
content_id         (VARCHAR)
sequence_order     (INTEGER)
recommendation_reason (VARCHAR)

TABLE: student_assignments
-----------------------------------------
student_assign_id  (UUID, Primary Key)
assignment_id      (UUID, Foreign Key)
student_id         (UUID, Foreign Key)
status             (ENUM: not_started, in_progress, submitted)
accommodations_id  (UUID)
accommodation_snapshot_json (JSONB)
assigned_track     (VARCHAR)
generated_from_smartscore (BOOLEAN)

TABLE: student_item_progress
-----------------------------------------
progress_id        (UUID, Primary Key)
student_assign_id  (UUID, Foreign Key)
item_id            (UUID, Foreign Key)
completion_pct     (FLOAT)
smart_score_start  (INTEGER)
smart_score_final  (INTEGER)
Delivery Architecture Diagram
The assignment system should create stable per-student records at publish time rather than recalculating everything live from class rosters.
This structure keeps assignment delivery stable, scalable, and auditable even when rosters change after publish.
Key implementation notes: Store the generated path, recommendation reasons, accommodation snapshot, assigned track, and starting SmartScore on the per-student assignment records. Keep raw time-on-problem telemetry outside the operational assignment tables and surface only summarized struggle indicators in reporting. Clever roster sync should support backfill for newly rostered students without rewriting completed work.

3d. Content Selection Interface

See the attachment ixl_demo.html. It replaces the static wireframe and serves as the clearest representation of the intended teacher workflow.

The demo should make one thing clear: teachers can either build homework manually for a student or use Auto-Assign to let IXL generate the first personalized draft from the data it already has.

Manual Build Mode

The teacher enters a student, reviews readiness, and manually selects skills, videos, and lessons. The left side of the builder supports search, standards-aligned filtering, and browsing across content types; the right side shows the ordered homework path, estimated student time, and whether the teacher is editing a group track or an individual-student version.

Auto-Assign Mode

Auto-Assign is the key feature in the demo. It analyzes the student's SmartScore, progress history, and other available IXL signals, then chooses the optimal mix of skills, lessons, and videos for a custom homework path. The point is not just "add content faster"; it is to turn IXL's existing student insight into publishable homework without rebuilding the work for every student.

Review State

Before publishing, the teacher should see the generated path, estimated time, why each item was recommended, applied accommodations, and whether edits affect a group or one student. For a K-5 teacher or interventionist, the highest-value path is often Auto-Assign followed by a quick review. For a secondary teacher, the better path may be manually editing the generated sequence, such as starting with a short lesson or video and then moving into targeted practice.

Publish Behavior

Publishing creates the stable student assignment records described in 3c. That means the teacher-approved path, SmartScore starting point, accommodation snapshot, and recommendation reasons are stored for reporting and auditability.

Most importantly, this should not feel like a content-picker bolted on top of IXL. It should connect three strengths IXL already has: content coverage, mastery data, and progress visibility.

3e. Integration and Distribution Priorities

Homework only becomes core workflow if it reaches students in the places where they already work. That makes integration a product requirement, not an implementation afterthought.

Integration Priority Rationale
Clever SSO and roster sync Phase 1 Roster-linked assignment delivery and backfill are foundational for district usability.
Chromebook / PWA homework entry Phase 1 Students need a visible homework destination, not one more hidden web task.
Google Classroom distribution Phase 2 Helps IXL homework appear alongside the rest of the teacher's digital assignment flow.

My view is that Clever-level compatibility is a launch requirement for district credibility. Broader distribution, like Google Classroom, can deepen adoption after the core homework object works. In other words, Homework is not just a content workflow; it is also a delivery workflow.

References

  1. Education Week: Here's How Many Hours a Week Teachers Work.
  2. Education Week: 8 Takeaways From a New Survey of Teachers.
  3. Education Week: How Teachers Spend Their Time.
  4. Merrimack College Teacher Survey.
  5. Clever School Solutions.
  6. Observed IXL workflow details are based on direct platform use and the attached prototype work in this submission.
